stem: In a medical treatment study comparing Drug X vs. Drug Y: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher recovery rate (72.7% vs 57.9%). However, when stratified by disease severity: In mild cases (n_X=40, n_Y=120): Treatment X achieves 87% vs Treatment Y's 82%. In severe cases (n_X=100, n_Y=30): Treatment X achieves 46% vs Treatment Y's 36%. The true population proportion of mild cases is uncertain, estimated between 41% and 59%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to severe cases (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.071, 0.079], point estimate: 0.075. Treatment X is genuinely better by 7.5 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=57.9%, Y=72.7% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In mild cases: X=87% > Y=82%. In severe cases: X=46% > Y=36%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Drug X is applied more to severe cases (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
